claim: Matuschak defines atomicity as one-concept-per-note that still captures the entirety of that concept, citing the Zettelkasten principle of separating concerns.
quote: It's best to create notes which are only about one thing—but which, as much as possible, capture the entirety of that thing. The underlying principle I'd call the principle of atomicity: put things which belong together in a Zettel, but try to separate concerns from one another.

claim: Matuschak argues notes should be factored by concept rather than by author, book, event, project, or topic so synthesis pressure and surprising cross-domain links emerge.
quote: It's best to factor Evergreen notes by concept (rather than by author, book, event, project, topic, etc). Novel connections tend to appear where they're not quite so expected. When arranging notes by concept, you may make surprising links between ideas that came up in very different books.

claim: Nick Milo's current LYT incarnation is Ideaverse Pro, which packages the ACE folder system (Atlas/Calendar/Efforts) plus the ARC ideation workflow (Add, Relate, Communicate) as the successor to the LYT Kit.
quote: Ideaverse Pro introduces the ARC framework – Add, Relate, and Communicate, which is a core ideation framework that guides ideas from conception to maturity.

claim: ACE separates a vault into three top-level folders — Atlas (knowledge), Calendar (time), Efforts (actions) — with a + inbox and X metadata folder, displacing topical folder trees.
quote: The ACE-x framework organizes a PKM system in folders for separate spaces for Atlas (knowledge), Calendar (time), and Efforts (actions), with a + (Plus) folder serving as an Inbox for new notes, and X folder for metadata.

claim: MOCs beat folders on four axes: a note can be linked from multiple MOCs, MOCs carry annotated commentary, restructuring is just editing wikilinks, and MOCs appear as nodes in graph view.
quote: A note can only live in one folder, but it can be linked from multiple MOCs simultaneously. Folders don't participate in Graph View. MOCs are real nodes in the knowledge network.

claim: Obsidian CEO Steph Ango runs an almost folder-free vault — most notes at root, only References/Clippings + hidden admin folders — and navigates by quick switcher, backlinks, and the categories property surfaced through Bases.
quote: I avoid folders because many of my entries belong to more than one area of thought. I try to always link the first mention of something.

claim: A widely-cited forum heuristic splits the decision: folders for project/output workflows, links for PKM, tags treated skeptically (the OP rejects them in favor of properties).
quote: Use a lot of folders if you're using Obsidian for managing writing projects, such as articles, blog posts, video scripts, etc. Use minimal folders if you're using Obsidian for building a personal knowledge management system.

claim: Eleanor Konik defends folders for cross-tool/automation backwards-compatibility and reserves tags for verb-based action metadata like #FollowUp, #addmoc, #articleseed.
quote: Folders have good backwards compatibility. Trying to use tags for everything makes tags less useful in my opinion, because then your tags get cluttered.

claim: The tags-as-status camp uses tags to qualify processing state (#unread, #idle, #processing, #shaped, #🌱 seedling, #🌲 evergreen) so the tag mutates without breaking the link graph.
quote: Status tags are useful to qualify your notes and keep track of your progress. By using tags for status, the tag can be easily changed as the status progresses, without impacting the connections of the note.

claim: The recommended hybrid splits tags (top-down 'is-a' classifiers, status, type) from topic notes/MOCs (bottom-up emergent hubs) and warns to wait for content density before promoting clusters into MOCs.
quote: You can think of tags as indicating 'is a' category, such as 'is a' book, article, task, etc. You can think of topics as a way to facilitate connections between notes. You have to create the content before you can map it.

claim: Past ~2k notes, manual MOC maintenance breaks down; the AutoMOC plugin auto-imports backlinks, tagged mentions, and aliases into the open MOC, and Backlink Cache speeds the Backlinks pane.
quote: The AutoMOC plugin streamlines managing MOC notes by ensuring that backlinks, tagged mentions, and alias references are automatically imported into the current note. The Backlink Cache plugin significantly speeds up the performance of the Backlinks Pane for large vaults.
